title: The Client Recovery SOP For Agencies
fileLabel: The Client Recovery SOP For Agencies
hook: I have lost three accounts to silence and one to a Slack message I should have made a phone call. This is the SOP I built after the fact: 10 conversation stages, 100 scripted lines, 6 ready-to-send templates, a severity routing matrix, and the channel-by-channel rules for when Slack works, when Loom backfires, and when only a phone call will save the account.
benefits: The 10-stage Recovery SOP: Acknowledgment, Ownership, Balanced Error, Explanation, Damage Assessment, Collaboration, Actions, Communication, Conclusion, Follow-Up. Each stage with 5 openers, 5 closers in agency voice, and the common pitfall operators step on.

100 scripted opener and closer lines you can lift word-for-word into your next recovery email, Slack thread, or Loom. Written for agency-owner-to-client voice, not corporate PR voice. Copy, adjust the names, ship.

A 3-tier severity routing matrix: Tier 1 minor frustration, Tier 2 actively pissed, Tier 3 account at risk. Each tier gets a channel choice, a response window, a written follow-up rule, and a check-in cadence. Stop guessing which fire deserves which response.

The 2026 channel decision rules: when Slack wins, when email wins, when only a Loom rebuilds trust, when only a phone call saves the account, and the 'no big calls before written notice' rule that has saved every Tier 3 account I have run.

6 ready-to-send templates with realistic copy: missed deadline (1-day, 3-day, week-plus late), below-quality work delivered, scope creep collision, communication blackout after going silent, blown campaign or launch, and the full Tier 3 written Recovery Plan template.

The 12 Mistakes To Never Make in a recovery conversation: using the word 'but', going silent, defensive language, blame-shifting, over-discounting before listening, promising what you cannot deliver, fixing-by-email when a call is needed, and 6 more. Each one paired with the corrective move.

The Recovery Tracking Scorecard: a one-page weekly check-in template, the 3 signals that mean the account is saved, the moment to declare full recovery, and the 30 / 60 / 90 follow-up cadence post-incident.

The When to Fire the Client Instead chapter. The 5 signals that mean recovery is no longer worth your time, the exit conversation framework, and the reference move on the way out so you part as professionals.
squeezeBullets: 10 recovery stages, 100 scripted opener and closer lines in agency-owner voice, copy and ship
Severity routing matrix: Tier 1, 2, 3. Channel, response window, follow-up rule, check-in cadence
6 ready-to-send templates: missed deadline, below-quality work, scope creep, blown launch, more
description:
Client conflict in 2026 is not what it was in 2018. Most of it now lives in writing. Slack, email, async Loom, the occasional Zoom. Which means the operator who can write a clean recovery message has an edge the operator on the phone used to have. And the operator who handles a recovery badly leaves a paper trail of it.

This SOP is the one I wish I had the first time a client sent me 'we need to talk' on a Sunday night.

Part 1, the Damage Assessment. The 3 client moods you have to diagnose before you reply, the 3 severity tiers (minor, actively pissed, account at risk), the 24-hour acknowledgment rule, and the first message you send no matter the tier.

Part 2, the 10 Stages of Recovery. Lead with a flowchart of the full sequence. For each stage, what it does, when to use it, 5 openers and 5 closers in agency-owner voice (every line rewritten, not the corporate originals), the common pitfall, and a real-world example. The 'no but' rule from the source is preserved as a callout because it is the most useful single rule in the whole document.

Part 3, 2026 Channel Decisions. Slack vs email vs Loom vs phone vs in-person. The Loom apology and when it backfires. The 'no big calls before written notice' rule. The phone-call-only-when scenarios.

Part 4, the Severity Routing Matrix. A 3-row table that tells you for each tier: the channel, the response window, the written follow-up rule, and the post-incident check-in cadence. Stop improvising under pressure.

Part 5, the Template Library. Six fill-in-the-blank templates that are realistic enough to ship: missed deadline (3 versions for 1-day, 3-day, week-plus late), below-quality work delivered, scope creep collision, communication blackout after going silent, blown campaign / blown launch, and the full Tier 3 written Recovery Plan. Realistic copy throughout. No pseudo-templates.

Part 6, the Recovery Tracking Scorecard. A one-page weekly check-in, the 3 signals that mean the account is saved, the threshold for declaring full recovery, and the 30 / 60 / 90 follow-up cadence after the incident closes.

Part 7, the 12 Mistakes To Never Make. From using the word 'but' to surprise discounts as guilt offerings. Each mistake named, diagnosed, and paired with the corrective move.

Part 8, When to Fire the Client Instead. The counterweight to the rest of the playbook. The 5 signals that mean recovery is no longer worth your time, the exit conversation framework, and the reference move on the way out.

Written for solo freelancers, agency owners, and in-house growth operators dealing with client conflict in 2026. If you have ever stared at a Slack message for an hour trying to figure out what to write back, this is the SOP you keep open while you draft the reply.

Included as a printable PDF and a duplicatable Notion reference.
emailSubject: Your Client Recovery SOP is ready
